_comment: The ideal closed-loop homestead OUTLINE (one person). Operator directives: 2026-07-05 'use Home as a page for outlining what we need in the perfect ideal 100% closed loop self-sustaining homestead'; same day: dual units 'metric (imperial)' so either background reads it instantly, plus TWO TIERS per loop: bare minimum vs a life of luxury with all the latest tech (assume comfort in space, not austerity). Numbers distilled from docs/design/homestead-solo-design.md (cross-checked against live game data); luxury-tier additions sized with honest engineering estimates. Every requirement carries tier: 'baseline' or 'luxury'. game_id is a REAL id in the game data (unit-test enforced) OR the empty string, which renders as a 'not in game yet' flag: the luxury tier doubles as the game-content gap list, exactly the operator's stated purpose. Rendered by BOTH the native Home page (src/gui/pages/homes.rs) and the web mirror (web/pages/home.html fetches /data/home_outline.json). Infinite-of-X: edit here, both surfaces update. Top-level data/ so the web deploy publishes it. No em dashes in copy (operator rule).
title: The ideal closed-loop homestead
subtitle: One person, seven loops, two tiers: the bare minimum that keeps you alive, and a life of luxury with all the latest tech. Both honestly sized, in metric and imperial.
intro: 100% closure is physically impossible (entropy always leaks), but a single person CAN close the survival loops: energy, water, air, climate, nutrients, and nearly all food. Each loop below lists the bare minimum first, then the luxury additions for living WELL, not just surviving; comfort in space is the design target, and anything the game does not have yet is flagged so this page doubles as the game's requirements list. What one home cannot close is listed last, on purpose: those gaps are why community and trade exist.

loops.name: Power
loops.demand: Bare minimum 4.0 kWh/day, duty-cycle honest (life support, water handling, cooking, cold storage, lights). A life of luxury runs about 16 kWh/day: add heating and cooling (about 5), hot water on demand (about 2), full fridge and freezer (about 1.5), washer and dryer (about 1), computers and entertainment (about 1.5), kitchen appliances, the 3D printer, and power tools.
loops.closure_note: Bare minimum: 4 panels yield about 5.76 kWh/day real (400 W x 4.5 sun-hours x 0.80 system losses), a 44% headroom. Luxury: 14 panels make about 20 kWh/day against the 16 kWh/day budget, with 8 battery banks (32 kWh, about 2 days of autonomy). Winter sun can halve; batteries plus the backup generator carry the gap, and the biodigester brews the generator's methane from your own waste.

requirements.why: 400 W each; 1.44 kWh/day real yield per panel, not nameplate
8 kWh usable, about 2 days of bare-minimum autonomy
0.7 kWh/day; keeps trickling when the sun does not
covers the winter deficit; burns biodigester methane
about 20 kWh/day real yield to fund the 16 kWh/day comfort budget
32 kWh, about 2 days of autonomy at full comfort

loops.name: Water
loops.demand: Bare minimum 80 L/day (21 gal/day) net household: drink, cook, wash. Luxury about 120 L/day (32 gal/day) with long hot showers, a washing machine, and a dishwasher. Irrigation is NOT net demand; greywater recycling covers it.
loops.closure_note: An 8,000 L (2,100 gal) cistern holds 100 days of bare-minimum autonomy, still 66 days at full luxury use, and passive rain catchment adds about 144 L/day (38 gal/day) for free. Dry-season storage is the binding constraint, so the oversized tank is a feature, not waste.

requirements.why: 8,000 L (2,100 gal) storage; rain catchment included
2 L/min (0.5 gal/min) lift refills a day's use in under an hour
240 L/day (63 gal/day) potable polish, 3x the one-person need
the tap/shower/sink node; cistern to pump to purifier to taps
heat-pump water heater, about 2 kWh/day for one person; endless hot showers
with a heat-pump dryer, about 1 kWh/day averaged
modern units use LESS water than hand washing, about 10 L (2.6 gal) per load

loops.name: Food
loops.demand: 2,200 kcal/day complete nutrition (about 55 g / 2 oz protein), one adult, both tiers; luxury adds variety, spice, and serious preservation, not more calories.
loops.closure_note: The honest cap is light, not shelf space: free sunlight is a single canopy, so a room's output is bounded by its lit floor area no matter how many hydroponic or aeroponic towers you stack. About 700 to 1,000 m2 (7,500 to 10,800 sq ft) of intensive growing feeds one person; the 34x34 m (112x112 ft) garden room is 1,156 m2 (12,440 sq ft), so the same garden that fell short for three people genuinely closes for one. Indoor alone reaches about 2,078 kcal/day (94%); one outdoor grain field plus one legume field push it into surplus, and the silo banks 750 days of buffer.

requirements.why: 960 kcal/day of bulk calories, aeroponic, NASA-real
828 kcal/day; all vegetables, vitamins, and plant protein across 32 real crops
75 kcal/day of fats (sunflower, flax)
100 kcal/day from dark vertical space; needs no light budget
closes B12 and omega-3, the two nutrients plants cannot give you
token indoor cereal; worst per-area indoor crop, kept minimal on purpose
3,100 kcal/day outdoor surplus, dried and stored
700 kcal/day protein AND fixes nitrogen for the grain field
750 days stored; the winter and bad-harvest buffer
about 1.5 kWh/day; fresh food, frozen harvest surplus, ice cream in space
spices, teas, chamomile, aloe; flavor is a luxury loop worth closing
extend the canopy past the sun IF the luxury power budget funds it; the honest teaching meter shows the cost

loops.name: Air
loops.demand: 0.55 kg/day (1.2 lb/day) of oxygen plus matching CO2 scrubbing (sealed-habitat sizing; on open Earth this loop is free ventilation), both tiers.
loops.closure_note: The garden is the primary life support: 1,156 m2 (12,440 sq ft) of plant canopy makes far more oxygen than one person needs. The 25 W recycler is the backup, and it is priority 1 on the power bus for a reason: cut power long enough and air is the first loop to fail.

requirements.why: 25 W, sized for three people, so one person has deep margin
the food loop IS the oxygen loop; bioregenerative and free
dust, pollen, and particulates out of the sealed air; allergy-free living

loops.name: Climate (heating and cooling)
loops.demand: Hold about 20 C (68 F) year-round through winter cold and summer heat. A sealed, well-insulated shell shrinks this loop to a fraction of what a normal house burns.
loops.closure_note: Insulation is the real machine: the shell does most of the work for free, passive solar gain and thermal mass carry the mild seasons, and a biomass stove burning your own woodlot covers deep winter at the bare minimum. Summer is shading, night ventilation, and the thermal-mass flywheel. The luxury heat pump moves 3 to 4 units of heat per unit of electricity, so even the all-electric path is efficient; its roughly 5 kWh/day seasonal average is the single biggest line in the luxury power budget, which is why it lives in that tier.

requirements.why: the highest-return component in the entire homestead; every other climate number scales off it
deep-winter heat from your own woodlot; ash goes to the composter
south glazing and mass floors store the day's heat for the night, no power at all
one machine does winter AND summer at 300-400% efficiency; about 5 kWh/day seasonal average
redundant spot cooling for heat waves or a server/workshop corner
warm feet, silent, runs off the heat pump's low-grade heat
schedules the whole loop around occupancy and the solar forecast

loops.name: Nutrients and waste
loops.demand: About 1.5 kg/day (3.3 lb/day) of organic waste (food scraps, crop residue, humanure) returned to the soil, both tiers.
loops.closure_note: This is the loop-closer: recycling waste lowers every other loop's demand. Compost plus urine diversion return 587% of the nitrogen, 533% of the phosphorus, and 197% of the potassium the crops need, so even the tightest nutrient is in surplus. Fish waste feeds the towers; the legume field fixes nitrogen; the biodigester turns the rest into generator fuel.

requirements.why: 1.2 kg/day (2.6 lb/day) nutrient plus 155 L/day (41 gal/day) greywater back to irrigation; the second bin bridges the 60-90 day maturation lag
blackwater handling for the wetroom
methane for the backup generator plus digestate fertilizer; waste becomes fuel

loops.name: Shelter and workshop
loops.demand: One insulated, walkable home wrapping every loop: garden room, wetroom, kitchen, workshop, storage, sleep. The luxury tier turns the workshop into a real fabrication bay.
loops.closure_note: The house is the container that makes every loop's numbers work: insulation shrinks the climate budget, the sealed envelope makes air a real loop, and room layout is the plumbing and power topology. The workshop is what keeps every other machine alive; at the luxury tier it also MAKES things: printed spare parts, custom brackets, repaired boards. Every wall, fixture, and furniture piece in the design is a real buildable id in the game today.

requirements.why: the room layout blueprint; the solo build uses fewer rooms of the same shells
sink, shower, toilet from the standard structures catalog
cook and preserve the harvest; cold storage is in the power budget
repairs keep every other loop's machines alive
about 0.2 kWh/day averaged; prints the brackets, knobs, and jigs that would otherwise be a supply run
about 0.5 kWh/day averaged across saws, drills, welder; hours become minutes
about 1.5 kWh/day; work, games, movies, and the home's own automation brain

cannot_close.note: Five loops stay open no matter how good the homestead is: electronics and semiconductors, metal from raw ore, medicine synthesis, equipment replacement, and raw chemistry inputs. They are listed in full in the panel below this outline. The gap is not a failure; it is why neighbors, trade, and civilization exist.

in_game_next: Play-load the solo home: walk into this exact build as a playable save (the data for it already exists as the home_solo variant).
Progress persistence: the Home page's live balances should track YOUR home as you build it, loop by loop, against this outline.
Author the luxury machines flagged 'not in game yet' on this page (on-demand water heater, dishwasher) so the comfort tier is fully buildable.
Real-home import: measure your actual house against the ideal (the long-term homes-as-profiles direction).
footer: Everything in the bare-minimum tier exists in the game's data today, and nearly all of the luxury tier does too. The outline IS the requirements list: when the in-game Home can be built, powered, watered, fed, breathed, climate-held, and composted to match this page at BOTH tiers, the Home feature is done enough to teach the real thing.
